design_philosophy.core_loop: Fly through eight elemental worlds, upgrade weapons and bombs via collection, manage ammo economy between levels using in-game currency, defeat progressively harder enemy waves and bosses
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (bomb ammo is limited) vs. upgrade pressure (needing to collect power-ups while dodging attacks); balancing weapon selection for different enemy patterns
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Space commander defending a colonized planetary system against cosmic-radiation-awakened threats; progression from weak single-hull fighter to multi-hull upgraded warship
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Unusual for NES shmups: permanent between-level shop system using collected money for upgrades rather than pure score chase; bomb ammo limitation creates strategic resource management absent in most arcade-style shooters; bizarre enemy design (robotic rabbits, happy faces, cigarettes) instead of military aesthetic

difficulty.lives_system: Standard lives system with ship durability mechanic: single-hull ship loses one life per hit; acquiring 'wing' upgrade allows two hits before loss of life, plus access to higher weapon upgrade tiers
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression with eight distinct areas; between-level shop and upgrade purchases persist across attempts
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: weapon selection (5 types), bomb selection (4 types), and upgrade strategy all player-determined; money economy allows purchasing power progression; wing acquisition gates advanced upgrades

progression.upgrade_system: Dual upgrade economy: (1) Weapon/bomb leveling via collecting matching numbered/lettered circles in succession (max 5 upgrades per weapon), higher levels consume more ammo; (2) Between-level shop purchases using collected money for hull/capability upgrades
progression.unlock_mechanism: Story-driven linear progression through 8 elemental planets (Moon, Fire, Water, Wood, Metal, Earth, Sun, Sun Interior); 'wing' acquisition unlocks larger ship hull and higher weapon tier caps; hidden Area 0 accessible via sound test
replayability_hooks: Hidden level select via sound test menu (Area 0 hidden stage)
Five weapon types with distinct mechanics encourage replay with different strategies
Money economy allows varied upgrade paths on replays
Regional differences (enemy designs, boss forms differ between JP/US) support discovery

world_building_techniques: Opening cinematic establishes sci-fi premise: Earth depleted, civilization moved to artificial IOTA Synthetica system
Elemental planet theming (each area tied to day-of-week element: Monday=Moon, Friday=Metal/Money, Sunday=Sun) creates mythological resonance
Stage names translated as celestial bodies with poetic framing
Ending epilogue subverts military narrative ('This isn't a sport') via commander's reflection
Japanese version includes extended worldbuilding: mention of mage, summoning ceremony, cosmic entity antagonist—substantially reduced in US release

boss_design.philosophy: Bizarre creature design matching strange-object enemy aesthetic; final boss takes multiple forms with significant regional censoring (Japanese version first form resembles baby; US version more grotesque)
notable_bosses: Area 2 mid-boss: 'value pack' matchbox (JP) vs. robotic object (US), fires projectiles
Area 8 final boss: Multi-phase creature with heavily censored first form; also appears mid-stage in different incarnation

faction_indicators: Galactic Federation (interstellar authority requesting hero intervention)
Commander Gun-Nac lineage (son of Legendary Xan—reference to earlier Compile game Zanac)
danger_signals: Cosmic radiation wave causing inanimate objects to animate and attack
Unknown mysterious cosmic entity controlling creature production
Progressive corruption: creatures 'grew more and more evil, eventually took over the factories'
environmental_storytelling: Planetary system IOTA Synthetica described as 'hip and up-scale' before catastrophe—contrast of beauty and technology under siege
Factory takeover progression visible through gameplay areas
Elemental world design (fire planet, water planet, etc.) suggests terraforming/colonization infrastructure

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor Gun-Nac article)
